items.q: What does dependency theory argue about development?
items.correct: Poorer 'periphery' countries are kept underdeveloped by their economic relationships with rich 'core' countries — through unequal trade, debt and historical colonialism. It challenges the view that integration with rich economies automatically helps.
items.misconception: Dependency theory means poor countries are simply lazy.
items.random_wrong: Dependency theory is about households relying on benefits.
items.why: Dependency theory is a structural critique of global capitalism, not a behavioural claim about poor countries. Students sometimes invert the analysis entirely or apply 'dependency' to individuals rather than nations.

items.q: What is the difference between a shift in demand and a movement along the demand curve?
items.correct: A movement along the curve is caused by a change in price; a shift of the whole curve is caused by changes in other factors (income, tastes, substitute prices).
items.misconception: A change in price shifts the demand curve.
items.random_wrong: Demand always falls when supply increases.
items.why: This is the single most common error in introductory economics. Price changes cause movements ALONG the curve; the curve itself shifts only when non-price factors change.

items.correct: The value of the next-best alternative foregone when a choice is made — the real cost of a decision in terms of what you give up.
items.misconception: Opportunity cost is the total of everything you give up when making a choice.
items.random_wrong: Opportunity cost is the money price of something.
items.why: Opportunity cost is the NEXT BEST alternative, not the sum of all alternatives. If you can do A, B or C and choose A, the opportunity cost is whichever of B or C was your second choice — not both.

items.q: In the standard prisoner's dilemma, the Nash equilibrium is:
items.why: It's Both confess. “Both stay silent” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: Best move regardless of opponent's choice
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Best move regardless of opponent's choice. “Must be played first” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Number of players in the classic prisoner's dilemma?
items.why: The real figure is 2. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
